meta.title: Broken Chair – Geneva, Switzerland
meta.description: Complete travel guide to Broken Chair in Geneva.

intro.title: Discover Broken Chair
intro.description: The Broken Chair is a monumental wooden sculpture located across the street from the Palace of Nations (United Nations European Headquarters) in Geneva, Switzerland. Designed by the Swiss artist Daniel Berset and constructed by carpenter Louis Genève, the 12-metre-high, 5.5-ton sculpture features a broken leg, symbolizing opposition to land mines and cluster bombs.

visitGuide.title: Visit Tips
items: The sculpture is located in a public square and is free to visit 24/7.
It's a perfect spot for photography, with the spectacular Palace of Nations fountains in the background.
The square is frequently used for peaceful assemblies and demonstrations.
Recommended to combine with a tour of the Palace of Nations (booking required) and the International District.

alsoKnownAs.title: Highlights
items: Symbol against landmines and cluster bombs
Located right next to the UN European Headquarters

sections.title: History & Origins
sections.content: The Broken Chair was originally commissioned by Handicap International in 1997 to urge all nations to sign the Ottawa Treaty banning landmines. It was initially planned to remain in front of the Palace of Nations for only three months, but due to its profound impact and strong public support, it was kept permanently.

sections.title: Symbolism
sections.content: The massive chair, made of 5.5 tons of wood, features a broken leg that represents the devastating injuries caused by landmines and cluster munitions. At the same time, its three solid legs and towering backrest symbolize the resilience and dignity of the victims, as well as humanity's hope for peace.

items.question: Do I need a ticket to see the Broken Chair?
items.answer: No. The Broken Chair is located on a public square (Place des Nations) in Geneva and is free to visit 24/7.

items.question: Can I visit at night?
items.answer: Yes, the square is illuminated at night and offers a more peaceful atmosphere. However, daytime provides better lighting for photography.

hours.title: Visiting Hours
hours.bestTimeSpring: Spring: Pleasant weather, perfect for walking along Lake Geneva
hours.bestTimeSummer: Summer: Sunny days, fountains are on, excellent for photography
hours.bestTimeAutumn: Autumn: Beautiful autumn scenery in Geneva
hours.bestTimeWinter: Winter: Can be combined with visits to nearby museums
hours.tip: When the fountains are on, they make a perfect photographic composition with the UN building and the Broken Chair.

tickets.title: Admission Information
tickets.parkingPrice: Hourly rates apply at the nearby underground parking (Parking des Nations)
tickets.guidedPrice: Guided tours of the UN Palace of Nations require advance booking on their official website

transport.title: How to Get Here
transport.fromCenterDesc: Take Tram 15 and get off at the 'Nations' stop. It is about a 1-minute walk from there.
transport.fromStationDesc: Take Tram 15 (direction: Nations) from outside the station and get off at the terminus 'Nations'. The journey takes about 10-15 minutes.
transport.fromAirportDesc: Take Bus 5 or 28 and get off at the 'Nations' stop. The ride takes around 15-20 minutes.
transport.publicTransportDesc: Geneva's public transport (TPG) is highly convenient. Tourists staying in Geneva hotels receive a free 'Geneva Transport Card' for unlimited rides on local transport. You can reach Place des Nations via Tram 15, or Buses 5, 8, 11, 22, and 28.
transport.walkingDesc: If you are around the Right Bank of Lake Geneva or the International District, walking is a great option. The Broken Chair is prominently visible at the Place des Nations.
transport.drivingDesc: There is an underground parking facility nearby (Parking des Nations). Since it is located in the international organization district, street parking can be limited, so public transport is highly recommended.

route.title: Recommended Route
steps: Admire and photograph the Broken Chair sculpture
Watch the fountain display on the square
Visit the UN European Headquarters (booking required)
Walk to the nearby International Red Cross and Red Crescent Museum

reviews.title: Visitor Reviews
reviews.declaration: Reviews can be viewed via Google Maps.
reviews.moreReviews: View more reviews on Google Maps

items.name: Sarah J.
items.text: A very powerful sculpture! Learning about its anti-landmine message makes it even more meaningful.

items.name: Mark T.
items.text: A must-see landmark in Geneva. It's right in front of the UN, and the fountains in the square are beautiful too.

items.name: Elena R.
items.text: Massive and spectacular. Standing underneath it gives you a strong visual impact.

items.name: David L.
items.text: A very educational piece of public art. Very easy to get to, the tram stops right there.

items.name: Paula W.
items.text: A symbol of peace and dignity. You must come and see it when in Geneva.

items.name: Sarai M.
items.text: Incredible wooden structure, and the message it conveys is very powerful. 100% recommended.

items.name: Kathy B.
items.text: Beautiful monument!! You'll definitely pass by it before taking the UN tour.

items.name: Thomas G.
items.text: Not just a photo spot, but an appeal for world peace. The atmosphere around it is great.

officialManagement.title: About Broken Chair
officialManagement.text: The Broken Chair was commissioned and is owned by Handicap International (now Humanity & Inclusion). The organization is an international NGO dedicated to supporting people with disabilities and vulnerable populations, with a particular focus on victims of landmines and cluster munitions. The sculpture's presence serves as a constant reminder to the international community of their commitments to the Ottawa Treaty and the Oslo Convention on Cluster Munitions.
